mnemonics.title: Algorithm Properties - FINDEA
mnemonics.content:
F - Finiteness (must terminate)
I - Input (zero or more)
N - Output (one or more)
D - Definiteness (unambiguous)
E - Effectiveness (basic operations)
A - All correct (produces correct output)

mnemonics.title: Loop Invariant Proof Steps - IMT
mnemonics.content:
I - Initialization (prove before first iteration)
M - Maintenance (prove iteration-to-iteration)
T - Termination (prove correctness at end)

mnemonics.title: Algorithm Design Paradigms - BDG
mnemonics.content:
B - Brute Force (try all)
D - Divide and Conquer (split-solve-combine)
G - Greedy (locally optimal)
P - Dynamic Programming (memoize subproblems)

acronyms.expansion: Time complexity as function of input size n
acronyms.usage: Used to analyze how algorithm runtime scales with input size. Example: T(n) = 3n² + 2n + 1 means quadratic growth.
Saying algorithm is O(n²) means it never grows faster than quadratic. Used for worst-case guarantees.
Saying problem is Ω(n log n) means no algorithm can solve it faster than n log n in the worst case.
Saying algorithm is Θ(n log n) means it's both O(n log n) and Ω(n log n) - exact asymptotic order.
Class of problems where solutions can be verified in polynomial time but finding solutions may require exponential time.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine graphs: constant (y=1), logarithmic (y=log n), linear (y=n), n log n (yours slowly rising), quadratic (y=n²), exponential (y=2^n) - each curve eventually dwarfs previous ones. Draw them to see crossover points.
For T(n)=2T(n/2)+n, draw tree: root level cost n, 2 subproblems with n/2 each, 4 subproblems with n/4 each. Height = log₂ n, total levels multiplied gives n log n total.

For insertion sort, visualize array as two zones: sorted (left) and unsorted (right). At each iteration, invariant holds: left portion is sorted. Draw array states before and after each iteration to see invariant in action.

Start → Is optimal substructure? No → Brute Force.
Yes → Can greedy choice be made? Yes → Greedy.
No or unsure → Overlapping subproblems? Yes → Dynamic Programming.
No → Divide and Conquer.

analogies.analogy: Algorithm is like a recipe (abstract instructions), program is like actually cooking the dish (concrete implementation). Same recipe can be implemented by different cooks in different languages.

If n is the number of pages in a book, O(n) is reading each page once; O(n²) is comparing every pair of pages - much slower for large books. The complexity measures how execution time grows as input grows.

Like a checkpoint in a relay race. At each handoff (iteration), certain conditions must hold (runner in correct position, baton passed). If invariant holds at start, is maintained through race, and at finish gives victory (correct output).

Like conquering a large kingdom by dividing into provinces, conquering each province separately, then unifying under one rule. Each province (subproblem) is smaller and more manageable than the whole kingdom.

Like finding shortest path in a maze. If you're at point X and need shortest path to exit, the optimal path from X must start with the optimal first step. The global optimal depends on local optimal choices.
